draft.introduction: The Sutta Nipāta is among the oldest strata of Buddhist literature. Its first chapter uses the image of a snake shedding its skin to describe the monk who sheds attachment.

translation.text: He who restrains his rising anger as one checks a rolling chariot—him I call a true charioteer. Others merely hold the reins. Overcome anger by non-anger, overcome evil by good, overcome the miser by giving, overcome the liar by truth.
translation.original_text: yo ve uppatitaṃ kodhaṃ ratthaṃ bhāntaṃ va dhāraye | tam ahaṃ sārathiṃ brūmi

translation.text: As a snake sheds its worn-out skin, so should one shed attachment to this world and the next. As a snake sheds its worn-out skin, so should one shed all craving.

translator_notes.note: The charioteer metaphor appears in the Katha Upanishad and Plato’s Phaedrus. Its presence in the earliest Buddhist texts suggests either direct borrowing from Upanishadic sources or a shared Indo-European heritage of the soul-as-chariot image.
